channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Nord Quantique Names Tobi Day-Hamilton CCO and Angela Olano VP of Marketing
item.description:
Curator's Take: Nord Quantique's strategic leadership additions signal the company's transition from pure R&D to market positioning as it prepares to commercialize its distinctive bosonic error correction approach. These executive hires typically precede major announcements or funding rounds, suggesting Nord Quantique is gearing up to make bolder claims about its path to practical quantum advantage. Day-Hamilton brings communications expertise from her time at Xanadu, while Olano's appointment indicates the company believes its technical differentiators are ready for broader industry messaging. The timing is particularly interesting given the intensifying competition among various error correction approaches, from surface codes to cat qubits, where clear communication of technical advantages will be crucial for market positioning.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Nord Quantique, a quantum computing company advancing efficient, scalable, and error corrected architectures, today announced the appointments of Tobi Day-Hamilton as Chief Communications Officer and Angela Olano as Vice President, Marketing. Nord Quantique has emerged as one of the most closely watched companies in the global quantum sector following recent demonstrations of quantum error […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: EPB Quantum to Host First Quantum in Business Event in Chattanooga
item.description:
Curator's Take: EPB Quantum's decision to host a business-focused quantum conference in Chattanooga signals the technology's shift from pure research to practical commercial adoption, particularly in traditionally conservative industries like finance and insurance. The $2.7 trillion economic value projection underscores growing confidence that near-term quantum applications can deliver real business value, even before fault-tolerant systems arrive. What makes this especially noteworthy is the venue choice: holding the event in a mid-sized southern city rather than traditional tech hubs like San Francisco or Boston suggests quantum computing's economic impact is expected to be geographically distributed. The focus on first-mover advantage reflects an industry maturation moment where companies need to start building quantum literacy and partnerships now rather than waiting for the technology to fully mature.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — On October 1, 2026, EPB Quantum will host the inaugural Quantum in Business (QiB) conference, exclusively in Chattanooga, Tennessee. QiB will highlight how companies can benefit from a projected $2.7 trillion in economic value by utilizing quantum computing in the near-term, especially in financial, healthcare, insurance, chemicals, logistics and automotive industries. What to know about QiB: “First movers who understand quantum computing’s business impact will shape tomorrow’s competitive landscape,” said […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SEEQC Secures Role in CHIPS Act-Funded NORDTECH Quantum Scaling Project
item.description:
Curator's Take: SEEQC's inclusion in the CHIPS Act-funded NORDTECH program represents a strategic convergence of quantum computing development with domestic semiconductor manufacturing priorities. This collaboration brings together academic heavyweights like Cornell and Princeton with commercial players including D-Wave, creating an ecosystem focused on the critical challenge of scaling quantum systems through advanced microelectronics integration. The involvement underscores how quantum computing is increasingly viewed as a national security priority requiring the same manufacturing infrastructure investments as classical computing. For SEEQC specifically, this validates their digital quantum computing approach that relies heavily on sophisticated cryogenic chip fabrication, positioning them alongside established quantum players in accessing federal research funding and fabrication resources.

Digital quantum hardware developer SEEQC, Inc. has announced its participation as a subcontractor in the multi-year Microelectronics Commons Northeast Regional Defense Technology Hub (NORDTECH) program. Funded via the CHIPS and Science Act, the project is led by NY CREATES and includes partners such as Cornell, Princeton, NYU, Syracuse, Quantum Circuits/D-Wave, and the Air Force Research [...] The post SEEQC Secures Role in CHIPS Act-Funded NORDTECH Quantum Scaling Project appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Phasecraft Appointed by ARPA-E to Advance Catalyst Simulation Frameworks for Hydrogen Electrolysis
item.description:
Curator's Take: This ARPA-E award represents a significant validation of quantum computing's potential to tackle real-world energy challenges, specifically optimizing catalysts for green hydrogen production, which is crucial for decarbonization efforts. Phasecraft's selection under the competitive QC3 program underscores a strategic shift in government funding toward quantum applications with near-term industrial impact rather than purely theoretical research. The substantial $4.5 million investment signals growing confidence that quantum algorithms are mature enough to address the complex chemistry simulations needed for catalyst design, which remains computationally intractable for classical systems. This project could serve as an important test case for whether current and near-term quantum hardware can deliver practical advantages in materials discovery for clean energy technologies.

Quantum software company Phasecraft Inc. has secured a $4,519,658 contract from the U.S. Department of Energy’s Advanced Research Projects Agency-Energy (ARPA-E). Awarded under the selective Quantum Computing for Computational Chemistry (QC3) program, the initiative is structured to develop, translate, and run highly optimized quantum simulation algorithms on emerging hardware. The project's primary operational mandate is [...] The post Phasecraft Appointed by ARPA-E to Advance Catalyst Simulation Frameworks for Hydrogen Electrolysis appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Agentic Operating System for Quantum R&D Teams
item.description:
Curator's Take: The Quantum Insider is exploring whether AI agents could transform how quantum R&D teams operate, addressing a crucial but often overlooked bottleneck in the field's progress. As quantum computing advances from theoretical breakthroughs to practical implementation, the coordination challenges of managing complex experiments, data analysis, and interdisciplinary collaboration have become increasingly apparent. This discussion comes at a pivotal moment when AI assistants are becoming sophisticated enough to handle specialized scientific workflows, potentially accelerating the timeline from quantum discovery to deployment. The intersection of agentic AI and quantum research could prove as important as the quantum algorithms themselves, especially as the industry scales toward commercial applications.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Are operating inefficiencies holding quantum research and development back? It’s an important question — one that’s being addressed by The Quantum Insider during an upcoming live panel discussion, “Are Agents the Secret to Quantum R&D Success?” on Tuesday, July 7. What does “agentic” actually mean inside a quantum workflow? Where […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: DARPA Selects Quandela For Stage A of The Quantum Benchmarking Initiative
item.description:
Curator's Take: DARPA's selection of Quandela, a French photonic quantum computing company, for its Quantum Benchmarking Initiative represents a significant validation of the photonic approach to quantum computing on the global stage. The initiative's focus on determining whether any architecture can reach utility-scale by 2033 sets a concrete timeline for the field's most pressing question, moving beyond laboratory demonstrations to practical applicability. Quandela's inclusion alongside presumably other leading platforms suggests DARPA is taking a technology-agnostic approach to evaluating which quantum computing methods might actually deliver on real-world problems first. The 2033 target date is particularly noteworthy as it provides the quantum computing community with a clear benchmark for transitioning from experimental systems to machines that can outperform classical computers on commercially relevant tasks.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quandela today announced it has been selected by the U.S. Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) to participate in Stage A of the Quantum Benchmarking Initiative, a multi-stage program designed to assess whether any quantum computing architecture can achieve utility-scale operation by 2033. Under Stage A, Quandela will present a detailed concept […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SEEQC Awarded Participation in U.S. CHIPS Act–Backed Microelectronics Commons NORDTECH Quantum R&D Program
item.description:
Curator's Take: SEEQC's inclusion in the CHIPS Act-backed NORDTECH program represents a significant federal endorsement of their energy-efficient quantum chip approach and signals growing government recognition that quantum computing infrastructure needs dedicated semiconductor manufacturing pathways. This four-year funding through the Microelectronics Commons hub system positions SEEQC to accelerate development of their cryogenic classical-quantum hybrid chips, which aim to solve one of quantum computing's thorniest problems: bringing control electronics closer to qubits without adding excess heat. The regional hub model is particularly strategic here, as it could help establish specialized quantum semiconductor fabrication capabilities in the Northeast, potentially reducing dependence on general-purpose fabs that aren't optimized for quantum applications. For an industry still finding its manufacturing footing, this kind of dedicated infrastructure investment could prove as important as the qubit breakthroughs that tend to grab headlines.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — SEEQC, Inc. (“SEEQC” or the “Company”), a developer and manufacturer of scalable, energy efficient digital chips for quantum computing systems, today announced its participation in a four-year Microelectronics Commons Northeast Regional Defense Technology Hub (NORDTECH) program. As one of eight U.S. Microelectronics Commons hubs, NORDTECH collaborates with regional partners across […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Researchers Clear Critical Hurdle For Electron-on-Helium Quantum Computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: Researchers at Princeton have achieved a critical milestone by demonstrating strong coupling between microwave photons and electrons floating above superfluid helium, opening a promising new avenue for quantum computing hardware. This electron-on-helium platform offers exceptional coherence times because the electrons are isolated in an ultra-clean environment, potentially outperforming traditional superconducting qubits in certain applications. The breakthrough shows that these floating electrons can now interact strongly enough with microwave resonators to enable quantum operations, moving the technology from theoretical curiosity toward practical implementation. While still in early stages compared to mainstream platforms, this achievement validates an alternative approach that could complement existing quantum computing architectures with superior noise resistance.

Insider Brief Researchers have demonstrated the strong coupling of a microwave photon to the motion of a single electron trapped above superfluid helium. The result, a long-sought milestone in quantum science, that the researcher suggest could advance efforts to build quantum computers using one of the field’s more unconventional hardware platforms. The study, published in […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: PNNL Discusses How Quantum Computing Could Impact Chemistry Research
item.description:
Curator's Take: Pacific Northwest National Laboratory's gathering of quantum computing leaders signals a crucial shift from theoretical promise to practical deployment planning, with chemistry applications positioned as a prime early use case for quantum advantage. The two-year timeline mentioned aligns with industry expectations for fault-tolerant systems capable of tackling molecular simulation problems beyond classical reach, though skeptics note such timelines have proven optimistic before. Chemistry research represents one of the most compelling near-term applications because quantum computers naturally model quantum mechanical systems, potentially revolutionizing drug discovery, materials design, and catalysis development. The convening of DOE leaders specifically around deployment strategy suggests federal agencies are moving beyond research funding into serious infrastructure and priority-setting for the quantum era.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — With the first practical quantum computers expected to arrive in about two years’ time, global quantum computing leaders are thinking about how these systems should first be deployed for the largest scientific impact. This spring, the Department of Energy’s Pacific Northwest National Laboratory brought together quantum computing leaders for the second annual Quantum […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Art Validates a Scalable Path to Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computing Using Multi-Qubit Gates
item.description:
Curator's Take: Quantum Art's validation of multi-qubit gates for fault-tolerant computing addresses one of the field's thorniest challenges: how to scale trapped-ion systems without error rates spiraling out of control. While most quantum computing approaches rely primarily on two-qubit gates, this research suggests that native multi-qubit operations could provide a more efficient path to fault tolerance, potentially reducing the massive overhead that currently makes practical quantum computers prohibitively large. The combination of microscopic noise modeling and fault-tolerance simulations is particularly notable, as it moves beyond theoretical proposals to examine real-world implementation challenges. This work could influence how the broader trapped-ion community thinks about architectural choices as the race toward commercially useful quantum computers intensifies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantum Art, a developer of full-stack, fault tolerant quantum computers based on trapped-ion qubits and a proprietary scale-up architecture, today announced research results verifying that its multi-qubit gate architecture advances scalable fault-tolerant quantum computing, validated through a detailed microscopic noise model and comprehensive fault-tolerance simulations. The company demonstrates its architecture […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: HPE Advances Quantum Computing at Scale with Expanded Industry Collaborations
item.description:
Curator's Take: HPE's expansion to eight quantum computing partnerships signals a strategic bet on hybrid classical-quantum systems becoming the near-term path to practical applications, rather than waiting for fault-tolerant quantum computers. This approach leverages HPE's existing supercomputing infrastructure to handle the massive classical computation needed to optimize and control quantum processors, addressing one of the field's biggest practical challenges. The move reflects growing industry consensus that useful quantum computing will emerge through tight integration with classical HPC rather than standalone quantum systems, potentially accelerating timelines for real-world applications in optimization, materials science, and drug discovery. While details remain sparse in the announcement, HPE's deep HPC expertise and customer relationships position them as a genuine bridge between today's classical computing and tomorrow's quantum capabilities.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — HPE (NYSE: HPE) today announced it has expanded relationships with eight companies to integrate high performance computing (HPC) and quantum computing systems to pave the way for practical and scalable hybrid classical-quantum applications in the future. “HPE is in a unique position to advance quantum computing.” As a global leader in […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Pulse Ventures’ Expanded Photonic Infrastructure Platform Boosts Optical Connectivity Via Scalable, Low Loss Integrated Optical Applications
item.description:
Curator's Take: Quantum Pulse Ventures' announcement of its expanded photonic platform addresses one of quantum computing's most persistent challenges: building scalable optical systems that minimize signal loss while maintaining high fidelity. The move beyond pure quantum computing components into quantum routing infrastructure suggests the company is positioning for a future where quantum networks connect multiple processors, not just individual machines. Photonic approaches have gained considerable momentum recently due to their room-temperature operation potential and natural compatibility with existing fiber networks, making this expansion particularly timely. However, the real test will be demonstrating these low-loss capabilities at scale, as many photonic quantum platforms have struggled to maintain performance when moving from lab prototypes to production systems.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantum Pulse Ventures (QPV) today announced a strategic expansion of its composite pulse photonics platform, to address scalable, low loss integrated optical applications with unmatched computation fidelity and accuracy for quantum computers and quantum routers. Quantum Pulse 2.0 (QP2.0) extends the company’s offering beyond optical quantum computing components, as part […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Guest Post: IBM’s Quantum Bet Is Really a Hardware Story
item.description:
Curator's Take: IBM's massive $10 billion commitment to quantum computing represents more than ambitious timelines—it signals a fundamental bet that quantum advantage will ultimately come down to solving hardware engineering challenges rather than purely algorithmic breakthroughs. The investment underscores how the race to fault-tolerant quantum computing has evolved from a scientific curiosity into an industrial-scale manufacturing problem, requiring the kind of sustained capital deployment typically seen in semiconductor fabs or aerospace development. While IBM's 2029 target for large-scale fault-tolerant systems is aggressive, this level of financial backing suggests the company believes the path from today's noisy intermediate-scale devices to practical quantum computers is now clear enough to justify betting serious money on execution rather than exploration.

Guest Post by Yoon Auh, Founder of BOLTS Technologies IBM’s recent announcement that it plans to invest more than $10 billion over the next five years to fund its quantum roadmap, including its stated goal of delivering a large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computer by 2029 has generated exactly the kind of headlines you would expect. Quantum […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Atom Computing and Phasecraft Partner on Material Science Algorithms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership bridges a critical gap in quantum computing by bringing together hardware and software specialists to tackle real-world materials science challenges. Phasecraft's track record in developing efficient quantum algorithms specifically optimized for near-term hardware could help Atom Computing's neutral-atom systems reach practical utility faster than either company could achieve alone. The focus on clean energy applications like battery and solar materials is particularly strategic, as these problems involve molecular simulations that quantum computers are theoretically well-suited to solve, potentially accelerating the timeline to commercial quantum advantage. This collaboration exemplifies the industry's shift from pure hardware development toward integrated hardware-software stacks targeting specific, high-value applications.

Neutral-atom quantum hardware developer Atom Computing and quantum software specialist Phasecraft have signed a Memorandum of Understanding to collaborate on application-focused algorithms. The partnership aims to adapt Phasecraft’s hardware-optimized software to Atom’s neutral-atom architecture, with a primary focus on accelerating materials discovery for clean energy applications like advanced batteries and solar technology. The joint effort [...] The post Atom Computing and Phasecraft Partner on Material Science Algorithms appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Cleveland Clinic and IBM Forum Highlights Advancements in AI and Quantum Computing for Healthcare Research
item.description:
Curator's Take: The Cleveland Clinic and IBM partnership represents a crucial proving ground for quantum computing's real-world healthcare applications, moving beyond theoretical promise to actual research implementation. This annual forum demonstrates how major healthcare institutions are actively integrating quantum and AI technologies into their research workflows, particularly important as the field transitions from pure R&D to practical problem-solving. The collaboration's third year signals sustained commitment rather than experimental dabbling, suggesting these advanced computing approaches are delivering tangible value in drug discovery and life sciences research. As quantum systems continue to mature, healthcare appears to be emerging as one of the first sectors where hybrid quantum-classical approaches can address problems that classical computing alone cannot efficiently solve.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The third annual Cleveland Discovery and Innovation Forum, hosted by Cleveland Clinic and IBM, highlighted progress in applying quantum computing and AI to healthcare and life sciences research. The forum brought together global leaders in healthcare, science and technology to share insights into how advanced computing is accelerating discovery and shaping the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Experimental quantum state learning with pairs of photons
item.description:
Curator's Take: This experimental work tackles a fundamental challenge in quantum measurement: distinguishing not just what mixed quantum state you have, but which specific pure states compose it and in what proportions. By exploiting the additional constraint that photons arrive in correlated pairs, the researchers bypass the usual ambiguity that plagues density matrix decomposition—essentially gaining access to information normally hidden behind quantum statistics. The demonstration shows impressive precision with just 10,000 photons achieving near-perfect tomography fidelity, suggesting practical applications in quantum communication protocols where knowing the exact preparation recipe matters, not just the average state. This pairing technique could prove valuable for characterizing quantum hardware and detecting subtle preparation errors that standard tomography would miss entirely.

Tomography allows one to estimate the density matrix describing the state an ensemble of quantum systems are prepared in (for example, polarization tomography determines the polarization state of a beam of identically prepared photons). In general, it is not possible to uniquely decompose the density matrix into its pure state components. Agarwal et al. proposed a protocol which, for a mixture composed of any two pure states of a qubit (with arbitrary probabilities), allows an observer to infer not only the density matrix but the identity of those specific pure states and their weights - the additional requirement being that the qubits arrive in pairs, where both qubits in each pair are in the same state. We experimentally demonstrate this learning-from-pairs concept using photons in the polarization degree of freedom. We use tomography to measure a sequence of single photons and make use of their time-of-arrival information to 'pair up' the photons after the measurement. From here we are able to infer the photons' polarization states and their respective probabilities, and we demonstrate this for various different choices of polarization states and ratios. Finally, we investigate our ability to discriminate between two equal mixtures of distinct pairs of orthogonal polarization states. We find that on the order of approx. 10e4 photons is typically enough to achieve tomography fidelities of approximately 0.9999. This is sufficient to discriminate between two different preparations of the same mixed state, differing by angles of less than 5 degrees between the pure states used in the two preparations.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Controlled Quantum Metrology with Anisotropic Heisenberg Spin Interactions under Intrinsic Decoherence
item.description:
Curator's Take: This theoretical study tackles a critical challenge in quantum sensing: maintaining measurement precision when quantum systems inevitably interact with their environment and lose coherence. The researchers demonstrate that carefully engineered anisotropic spin systems with Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions can remain effective for precision measurements even under decoherence, essentially finding sweet spots where quantum advantages persist despite noise. What makes this particularly valuable is the practical roadmap it provides for experimentalists working with spin-based quantum sensors, showing exactly how to tune system parameters to optimize performance for specific sensing tasks like magnetic field detection. The connection drawn between quantum resources like entanglement and coherence with actual metrological performance helps bridge the gap between abstract quantum advantages and real-world sensor applications.

We theoretically investigate quantum parameter estimation in a two-qubit anisotropic Heisenberg spin system with Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction in the presence of intrinsic decoherence described by the Milburn model. Using the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), we study the estimation of both the uniform magnetic field and the DM interaction strength. Analytical expressions for the time-evolved density matrix are obtained and used to explore the effects of exchange anisotropy, intrinsic decoherence, and probe-state preparation on the achievable estimation precision. Our results show that suitable tuning of the anisotropic exchange coupling and the initial entangled state can considerably enhance the estimation performance, with different optimal parameter regimes emerging for magnetic-field and DM-interaction sensing. To better understand the role of quantum resources in metrology, we also examine the behaviour of concurrence, quantum coherence, and von Neumann entropy. Overall, our findings demonstrate that anisotropic Heisenberg spin systems with DM interaction provide a promising and flexible platform for high-precision quantum metrology even in the presence of intrinsic decoherence.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantifying Coherence-to-Entanglement Conversion Efficiency under Noisy Operations
item.description:
Curator's Take: This theoretical work provides precise mathematical tools for understanding a fundamental challenge in quantum computing: how efficiently we can convert quantum coherence in single qubits into the entanglement needed for multi-qubit operations when realistic noise is present. The finding that maximally coherent inputs are not just better but actually optimal for fighting depolarization noise offers concrete guidance for gate design and error mitigation strategies. Particularly valuable is the identification of qualitatively different degradation patterns between phase damping and depolarization, which could help engineers diagnose noise sources in real quantum processors and tailor their error correction approaches accordingly. The closed-form solutions make these insights directly applicable to hardware characterization without requiring expensive numerical simulations.

We investigate the noise-limited conversion of local quantum coherence into bipartite entanglement in a minimal two-qubit protocol comprising a coherent single-qubit input, an incoherent ancilla, an ideal CNOT operation, and subsequent environmental noise. Employing the $l_1$-norm of coherence and the entanglement negativity as resource quantifiers, we establish an exact closed-form correspondence between local single-qubit input coherence and the two-qubit entanglement generated in the noiseless limit, showing that the output negativity is precisely one half of the initial $l_1$-coherence. We then derive analytic expressions for the surviving entanglement and the associated coherence-to-entanglement conversion efficiency under two representative noise mechanisms: independent phase damping and global two-qubit depolarizing noise. The two channels exhibit qualitatively distinct degradation behavior. Phase damping induces a universal multiplicative suppression of the generated entanglement, yielding a coherence-independent conversion efficiency and no finite-noise entanglement sudden death. In contrast, global depolarization introduces an isotropic mixing contribution that shifts the partial-transpose spectrum, producing coherence-dependent degradation and a finite sudden-death threshold. We show that maximally coherent inputs not only maximize the entanglement generated by the CNOT protocol but also optimize its robustness against depolarizing noise. Direct density-matrix simulations validate the analytic results to numerical precision. These findings provide a compact analytic benchmark for assessing how different noise mechanisms constrain coherence-to-entanglement conversion in elementary quantum-information protocols and near-term quantum devices.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: 3D Ising criticality with Platonic lattice superconducting qubits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work demonstrates a clever use of quantum hardware to tackle one of statistical physics' most stubborn problems: precisely determining critical exponents in the 3D Ising model, which has resisted analytical solutions for decades. The researchers cleverly mapped this challenge onto just 9 superconducting qubits arranged in a Platonic lattice geometry, using conformal field theory connections to extract critical exponents that match theoretical predictions to impressive precision. What makes this particularly significant is that it shows how even modest-sized quantum processors can probe fundamental physics questions when equipped with the right algorithmic approach, here combining variational methods with phase-based optimization and spectral analysis. The achievement hints at a sweet spot where near-term quantum devices can complement classical simulations in studying critical phenomena and phase transitions.

The three-dimensional (3D) Ising model is a foundational model in statistical physics and critical phenomena, yet its analytical intractability has long impeded the precise determination of universal critical exponents. While high-precision estimates have been obtained through classical numerical methods and conformal bootstrap techniques, a direct quantum simulation of the 3D Ising criticality remains challenging, requiring nontrivial connectivity, sufficient system size, and high spectral resolution. In this work, assisted by the state-operator correspondence of conformal field theory, we perform a digital quantum simulation of the 3D Ising critical exponents using a multiply-connected 9-qubit superconducting quantum processor with a Platonic lattice geometry. Employing an extended variational quantum eigensolver equipped with a phase-based loss function, we variationally prepare the low-energy eigenstates of the transverse-field Ising model on a cubic Platonic lattice encoded in an 8-qubit register. The four lowest eigenenergies are extracted via Fourier-transform analysis and high-precision numerical fitting, agreeing with the exact diagonalization values up to +/- 0.001. The resulting scaling dimension Delta_epsilon = 1.5850 and critical exponent nu = 0.7067 match well with theory.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: WiMi Develops Quantum Kernel Convolution Method for NISQ Quantum Devices
item.description:
Curator's Take: WiMi's quantum kernel convolution approach represents a pragmatic step toward making quantum machine learning work on today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices rather than waiting for fault-tolerant systems. The key innovation lies in adapting convolutional neural network operations—which have revolutionized classical image processing and pattern recognition—to run within the severe constraints of current quantum hardware, including limited qubit counts and high error rates. While press releases from publicly traded companies often promise more than they deliver, any genuine progress in NISQ-era quantum CNNs could accelerate practical applications in image classification and data analysis where quantum advantage remains elusive. This work joins a growing body of research attempting to extract useful computation from imperfect quantum processors, though independent validation and performance benchmarks against classical methods will be essential to assess its real-world impact.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — WiMi Hologram Cloud Inc. (NASDAQ: WiMi) (“WiMi” or the “Company”), a leading global Hologram Augmented Reality (“AR”) Technology provider, announces the release of a core technology for hybrid Quantum Convolutional Neural Network (QCNN), proposing and implementing a Quantum Kernel Convolution (QKC) scheme capable of running on current noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Physically Motivated Ansatz for Open Fermionic Systems on Quantum Computer
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's trickier challenges: simulating open quantum systems that exchange energy and particles with their environment, which is crucial for understanding real-world materials and chemical reactions. The researchers' physically motivated ansatz dramatically outperforms standard hardware-efficient approaches by avoiding the notorious barren plateau problem, where optimization landscapes become impossibly flat. The ten-orders-of-magnitude improvement in accuracy is particularly striking and suggests this method could enable practical simulations of strongly correlated fermions far from equilibrium, a regime that classical computers struggle with and where phenomena like high-temperature superconductivity live. This represents meaningful progress toward making variational quantum algorithms work reliably for open systems, not just the closed-system problems that dominate current research.

Determining non-equilibrium steady states (NESS) of open fermionic systems is a fundamental problem akin to finding ground states of closed systems. To address this, variational quantum algorithms can be used to solve the Lindblad master equation, much like the Schrödinger equation, yet ansatz design for NESS remains challenging. Existing approaches rely mostly on hardware-efficient ansätze (HEA), which suffer from the barren plateau problem. Here, we introduce a physically motivated ansatz named NE-UCC. Numerical simulations demonstrate that NE-UCC reliably converges to the steady state even in strongly correlated regimes far from equilibrium, reducing the infidelity by up to ten orders of magnitude compared to HEA. Furthermore, NE-UCC facilitates the exploration of excited eigenmodes with specific symmetries.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UK, Japan Expand Quantum Partnership With Focus on Commercial Deployment
item.description:
Curator's Take: This bilateral agreement marks a strategic shift from laboratory quantum research toward real-world deployment infrastructure, suggesting both nations view commercial quantum applications as imminent rather than distant. The partnership's emphasis on industrial coordination and infrastructure integration addresses a critical gap in quantum development — the challenging transition from academic breakthroughs to market-ready products and services. By aligning quantum priorities with AI and cybersecurity, the UK and Japan are positioning quantum technologies within the broader context of national technological competitiveness, potentially accelerating standardization efforts and creating clearer pathways for quantum startups and established tech companies to bring products to market. This represents the kind of government-level commitment that could help transform quantum computing from a promising research field into a functional industrial sector.

Insider Brief The United Kingdom and Japan are expanding their quantum partnership beyond research collaboration and toward commercialization, infrastructure integration and long-term industrial coordination. The two countries on Sunday unveiled a new Frontier Technology Partnership that places quantum technologies alongside artificial intelligence, cybersecurity and advanced communications as priority areas for joint action. The agreement signals […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Motion Establishes Silicon CMOS Hardware Base within Discovery District Maryland
item.description:
Curator's Take: Quantum Motion's strategic move to Maryland's Discovery District represents a significant validation of silicon spin qubits as a competitive quantum computing platform, particularly since the company is positioning itself alongside major players like Microsoft and IQM. The choice of silicon CMOS technology is especially noteworthy because it leverages existing semiconductor manufacturing infrastructure, potentially offering a faster path to scalability compared to platforms requiring exotic materials or extreme cooling. This clustering of diverse quantum hardware approaches in one location could accelerate cross-pollination of ideas and talent, while also signaling that the industry is moving beyond pure research into serious commercialization efforts that require dedicated engineering facilities.

Silicon spin-qubit developer Quantum Motion has finalized an agreement to establish an engineering facility within the Capital of Quantum (CoQ) deep-tech complex located in Discovery District Maryland. The United Kingdom-headquartered firm joins an established hardware cluster that houses IQM’s primary United States Quantum Technology Center and Microsoft’s Quantum Research Center. The regional expansion is designed [...] The post Quantum Motion Establishes Silicon CMOS Hardware Base within Discovery District Maryland appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Nonlocal Games on Graph Ensembles
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a significant leap from theoretical quantum advantage to practical implementation by demonstrating that entangled quantum systems can help mobile agents coordinate movements on uncertain networks better than any classical approach—and they've proven it with actual physically separated ion-trap systems, not just simulations. What makes this particularly intriguing is the counterintuitive finding that giving quantum players more local information actually increases their advantage over classical strategies, suggesting fundamentally different coordination mechanisms at play. The work bridges an important gap between abstract nonlocal game theory and real-world applications like robotic swarm coordination or distributed sensing, where agents must make decisions with incomplete network knowledge. By moving beyond idealized conditions and single-processor simulations, this research charts a concrete path toward practical quantum advantages in collective decision-making scenarios.

Quantum entanglement is one of the most striking discoveries in all of science. This effect allows, for instance, two spatially separated agents to coordinate their actions, without communication, to an extent that is both counter-intuitive, and provably impossible by any other physical means. A recently discovered example is that of mobile agents (players) performing spatial coordination tasks such as rendezvous, where the agents aim to meet on a network without communication. Until now, demonstrations of this advantage have relied on highly idealized conditions: agents are assumed to have complete knowledge of the topography, and experiments have been restricted to simulations using data generated by qubits within a single quantum processor. Here we address both limitations by developing a theory for graph ensembles that capture topographical uncertainty and by experimentally demonstrating the advantage in rendezvous scenarios between physically separated ion-trap systems with access to remote entanglement. Moreover, we simulate a broader set of problems on superconducting hardware. Surprisingly, when players are given the ability to gather more local information the quantum advantage increases -- a feat impossible by classical means. Our findings establish a concrete route toward practical quantum advantages in motion coordination problems. More broadly, they point to a new way of using portable quantum devices to enhance collective decision-making in uncertain environments.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuEra Outlines 2028 Roadmap for 256-Logical-Qubit “Libra” System and Expanded AWS Cloud Partnership
item.description:
Curator's Take: QuEra's ambitious 2028 roadmap marks a significant milestone in the race toward practical quantum computing, with their Libra system targeting 256 logical qubits—a scale where genuinely useful quantum algorithms could begin to outperform classical computers on real-world problems. The partnership with AWS is particularly noteworthy because it signals growing confidence from major cloud providers that fault-tolerant quantum computing is transitioning from theoretical possibility to concrete engineering challenge. The "megaquop" classification (referring to quantum operations per second) suggests QuEra expects not just stable logical qubits, but the high-speed gate operations necessary for complex computations. While 2028 remains four years away and quantum hardware timelines have historically slipped, the specificity of this roadmap and AWS backing suggest neutral atom platforms are maturing into serious contenders alongside superconducting and trapped-ion approaches.

Neutral-atom hardware developer QuEra Computing and Amazon Web Services (AWS) have announced an expanded, multi-year strategic collaboration to bring the first fault-tolerant quantum computer to the cloud. Scheduled for release in 2028, QuEra's upcoming system, Libra, is designed as a "megaquop-class" processor. This classification indicates that the hardware is engineered to execute on the order [...] The post QuEra Outlines 2028 Roadmap for 256-Logical-Qubit “Libra” System and Expanded AWS Cloud Partnership appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Capital of Quantum Welcomes Quantum Motion to Discovery District Maryland
item.description:
Curator's Take: Quantum Motion's expansion to Maryland's Discovery District signals the growing gravitational pull of regional quantum hubs beyond traditional tech centers. The UK-based company's silicon CMOS approach is particularly noteworthy because it leverages existing semiconductor manufacturing infrastructure, potentially offering a more scalable path to large-scale quantum computers than exotic materials requiring extreme conditions. Maryland has been strategically positioning itself as America's quantum capital through coordinated investment in quantum startups, federal research partnerships with nearby agencies like NIST, and university programs, creating a geographic clustering effect similar to what happened with biotech in Boston. This move reflects a broader trend of quantum hardware companies establishing international footprints to tap into diverse talent pools and funding ecosystems while staying close to government customers and research collaborators.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE – The Capital of Quantum (CoQ) announced today that Quantum Motion, a U.K. company that develops full-stack silicon CMOS quantum computers, will establish a facility in Discovery District Maryland. Quantum Motion joins a growing roster of global quantum leaders at the heart of the nation’s most dynamic quantum ecosystem. CoQ is building out dedicated […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Passive quantum error correction doubles qubit lifetime, reaching break-even point
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a fundamental shift in how we approach quantum error correction by turning a bug into a feature. Instead of fighting energy dissipation with complex active correction schemes that require constant measurement and feedback, this passive approach harnesses the natural physics of the system to automatically stabilize qubits. Reaching the break-even point where protected qubits outlast unprotected ones is a critical milestone, and doubling qubit lifetime suggests this technique could significantly reduce the overhead that has made error correction so resource-intensive. If scalable, passive correction could accelerate the timeline for practical quantum computers by eliminating much of the complex control infrastructure that currently dominates quantum hardware designs.

A team of U.S. researchers has designed a passive quantum error correction technique that enables qubits to correct their own errors. Demonstrated by Shruti Shirol and colleagues at the University of Massachusetts Amherst, the protocol transforms the inevitable dissipation of energy in qubit systems from a hindrance into an advantage, offering a promising route toward practical quantum computing outside the lab. The research has been published in Physical Review X.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: AWS Deepens Strategic Collaboration with QuEra to Bring Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computing to Amazon Braket
item.description:
Curator's Take: AWS's partnership with QuEra to deliver fault-tolerant quantum computers on Amazon Braket by 2028 represents a significant milestone in making practical quantum computing accessible through cloud infrastructure. This announcement signals growing confidence that error-corrected quantum systems capable of running scientifically meaningful applications are now on a realistic timeline rather than remaining perpetually decades away. The strategic collaboration extends AWS's multi-platform approach on Braket, allowing researchers to prepare algorithms and applications for fault-tolerant hardware before it arrives. While 2028 may seem distant, this timeline actually aligns with the aggressive roadmaps of leading quantum companies and suggests the industry is converging on when the technology will transition from interesting demonstrations to solving real-world problems that classical computers cannot handle.

AWS and QuEra will bring the first fault-tolerant quantum computers to the cloud, enabling scientifically relevant applications starting in 2028. Since launching Amazon Braket in 2020, AWS has provided customers access to quantum computing hardware across multiple modalities, helping them explore quantum algorithms and prepare for the future of accelerated computing. Today, we are announcing […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum enhancement and Doppler suppression of Kasevich-Chu atom interferometer with motional squeezing states
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a persistent challenge in quantum sensing by demonstrating that motional squeezing—squeezing the quantum uncertainty in atomic motion rather than internal spin states—can enhance atom interferometer sensitivity up to four times beyond classical limits while remaining remarkably robust against Doppler broadening effects. The work is particularly significant for practical applications like gravimeters on moving platforms (ships, aircraft, vehicles), where traditional spin-squeezing approaches fail due to motion-induced decoherence that destroys the delicate entanglement between atoms. By providing a complete theoretical framework with optimal control parameters for different measurement protocols, the authors offer a roadmap for next-generation quantum sensors that could maintain quantum advantages in real-world, noisy environments. The discovery that quantum enhancement can actually dominate over Doppler suppression in certain regimes suggests these techniques might enable precision measurements in scenarios previously thought impractical for quantum-enhanced sensing.

Hybridization of internal and external atomic degrees of freedom in a Kasevich-Chu interferometer enables the possibility to enhance the sensitivity significantly even under quantum-standard limit. By introducing motional squeezing state as an input, we systematically derive the computational framework of quantum and classical Fisher information of two measurement protocols for arbitrary strength of Doppler effects. Through maximizing the corresponding classical Fisher information, we obtain the optimal control parameters and the corresponding quantum Fisher information. For population measurement, the largest sensitivity can be as large as four times than the semi-classical limit through enlarging the atom coherence length. For joint measurement of population and position, the competition between quantum enhancement and Doppler suppression induces two three behaviors, in one regime, the quantum enhancement dominates even in presence of strong Doppler broadening effects where the sensitivity is significantly enhanced; while in another regime, an optimal squeezing parameter is observed where the classical Fisher information reaches the maximum. Our results clearly demonstrate the robustness of external quantum enhancement against Doppler suppression. Our proposal can be readily applied to gravimeter of mobile platform where decoherence from noise will damage the many-body entanglement of internal spin squeezing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Fuzzy-processing quantum computation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This paper introduces an intriguing alternative to traditional quantum error correction by borrowing concepts from fuzzy logic, potentially offering a more resource-efficient path forward for near-term quantum computers. Rather than demanding perfect qubit measurements and immediate corrections, the fuzzy processing framework tolerates uncertainty by encoding information across probability distributions and making decisions based on approximate state recognition. The approach appears particularly well-suited for optimization and decision problems where exact answers aren't always necessary, potentially enabling useful quantum computations on today's noisy devices without the massive overhead of full error correction. While the practical performance remains to be demonstrated experimentally, this represents a creative middle ground between error-prone NISQ devices and fault-tolerant quantum computers that could accelerate real-world applications.

Quantum computation has attracted numerous attentions and develops rapidly in the recent decades. To against the decoherence and the control errors upon the qubits, quantum error corrections are adopted. Such approaches require lots of redundant qubits, accurate measurement and timely feedback. Here we investigate a new framework of quantum computation that is associated with fuzzy processing. It will benefit significantly from three aspects: the fuzzy recognition of qubit states reduce the required gate fidelity; the fuzzy encoding encodes the information of the qubits into a distribution of probability, suppressing the fluctuations in the output of long quantum circuits; the fuzzy feedback offers a more efficient way to control the qubits when precision information of quantum states are absent. Furthermore, the fuzzy processing can be integrated into quantum error correction, eliminating the need for immediate correction operations. The proposed scheme will be fairly suitable for the solution of decision problems, which has significant applications in the optimization problems and control problems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Electronic Band Structure of Silicon Determined via a Variational Adiabatic Eigensolver: Theory and Experiment
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a significant step toward making quantum computers useful for real materials science by successfully calculating silicon's band structure on actual quantum hardware. The hybrid approach cleverly sidesteps two major obstacles that have plagued NISQ-era quantum simulations: the difficulty of building deep adiabatic circuits and the poor performance of standard variational methods on excited states. Achieving agreement within 0.01 eV of theoretical predictions for silicon—arguably the most important semiconductor in existence—demonstrates that near-term quantum devices can already provide chemically meaningful results for materials problems. The momentum-space truncation technique that enables this calculation with modest qubit counts could prove especially valuable as researchers work to transition quantum advantage from carefully crafted benchmark problems to industrially relevant materials challenges.

This work addresses the critical challenge of excited-state preparation for semiconductor band structure calculations. We introduce a variational adiabatic eigensolver (VAE) protocol that combines adiabatic evolution with variational optimization to prepare high-fidelity eigenstates on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices. Applying a momentum-space truncation, we accurately compute the electronic band structure of silicon -- an idealized infinite periodic system -- using only a modest number of qubits. Our approach employs multi-qubit parameterized circuits and a phase-based loss function, overcoming limitations of conventional methods. These limitations include the circuit-construction difficulty in traditional adiabatic approaches and the reduced accuracy of variational quantum eigensolvers for excited states. Through rigorous numerical simulation and experimental implementation on a superconducting quantum processor, we successfully prepare silicon's valence-band and conduction-band eigenstates. Single-shot readout yields state fidelities exceeding 96%, and the measured energy expectations agree with theoretical band energies within 0.5 eV. Further refinement via single-frequency oscillation fitting reduces the energy deviation to below 0.01 eV. This framework provides a robust and practical pathway for precisely determining electronic structures in quantum materials.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: What does measuring one qubit reveal about another? $K$-networks as a directed diagnostic for quantum circuits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This paper introduces a clever diagnostic tool that addresses a fundamental challenge in quantum computing: understanding how information flows through multi-qubit circuits. While traditional metrics summarize quantum correlations symmetrically, the K-network reveals directional relationships—specifically, how measuring one qubit in a particular basis affects what we can predict about another qubit's state. The approach is particularly valuable because it captures circuit structures like feedforward operations and phase relationships that conventional measurement-basis summaries miss, making it useful for debugging and understanding algorithms from Grover search to QAOA. By computing these directed networks from accessible two-qubit density matrices rather than full many-qubit states, the authors provide circuit designers with a practical lens for visualizing quantum information flow without the exponential cost of complete state tomography.

Many-qubit circuit states are hard to inspect directly, so they are often summarized by pairwise graph weights. Common pairwise weights report symmetric correlations, while many circuit questions are directed and basis-specific: if qubit $i$ is measured in a given basis, how strongly does the outcome reshape the conditional state of qubit $j$? We define $K_{i\to j}$, a directed, basis-conditioned edge weight for this question. It is large when the two measurement outcomes occur with comparable probability and leave qubit $j$ in clearly different conditional states; it is zero when the source outcome is deterministic or the target states are indistinguishable. The scalar uses standard binary-ensemble distinguishability; the paper's contribution is to turn this conditional comparison into a directed network layer for circuit states. The resulting networks are computable from two-qubit reduced density matrices. They are diagnostic (not entanglement measures): for pure two-qubit states $K$ reduces to the tangle $C^2$ (squared concurrence)~\cite{WoottersConcurrence,CKWTangle}, while separable mixed states can reach $K=1$. Examples on teleportation, Grover, QAOA, and random circuit families show the intended use: $K$-networks map feed-forward, phase, and interaction-graph structure that symmetric or computational-basis summaries can leave weak or absent.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Preparation of Fractional Quantum Hall States on Quantum Computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work addresses a longstanding challenge in quantum simulation by developing efficient quantum circuits to prepare exotic fractional quantum Hall states, which exhibit bizarre phenomena like particles with fractional charge and topological order that could enable robust quantum computing. Unlike previous methods that required slowly evolving complex Hamiltonians or were limited to simplified one-dimensional geometries, this approach uses direct circuit construction that works for realistic two-dimensional systems while dramatically reducing the computational overhead. The team went beyond theory by designing specific control pulses for today's superconducting and Rydberg atom quantum computers, making this genuinely implementable on near-term hardware. This bridges a critical gap between condensed matter physics and quantum computing, potentially enabling experimental verification of exotic quantum states while advancing our toolkit for quantum simulation on current and future devices.

The realization of fractional quantum Hall (FQH) states, characterized by fractional charge and intrinsic topological order, on quantum computers represents a central challenge at the interface of condensed matter physics and quantum information science. Current methods are grouped into two types: methods based on (quasi-)adiabatic evolution of complex parent Hamiltonians to yield target states, and circuit-based approaches for direct state preparation, which are confined to effectively one-dimensional systems near the thin cylinder or torus limit. We introduce a complementary scheme relying on direct quantum circuit construction, which works for arbitrary geometries. Specifically, we present a method to precisely prepare the $ν=1/3$ Laughlin state on the sphere geometry and demonstrate that it significantly reduces the required number of two-qubit gates and circuit depth, compared to variational quantum circuit approaches. In addition, we employ optimal control techniques to design control pulses for both superconducting and Rydberg atom platforms, identifying experimentally feasible protocols for state preparation. Our results provide an efficient and hardware-relevant pathway for realizing generic FQH states on both noisy intermediate-scale and fault-tolerant quantum devices.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuiX Quantum Joins QuantumBW and Photonics BW Networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights the growing importance of regional quantum ecosystems as companies recognize the value of localized collaboration networks. QuiX Quantum's decision to join these Baden-Württemberg networks reflects a broader trend where quantum hardware companies are strategically positioning themselves within European innovation hubs to accelerate technology transfer and forge industry partnerships. The move is particularly significant given Baden-Württemberg's emergence as a quantum powerhouse, home to major research institutions and a thriving photonics industry that's crucial for photonic quantum computing platforms like those QuiX develops. This type of regional clustering could prove essential for bridging the gap between quantum research and commercial applications across Europe.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuiX Quantum is pleased to announce its membership in QuantumBW and Photonics BW, two leading Baden-Württemberg networks driving collaboration across quantum technologies, photonics, research, industry and technology transfer. As a Dutch-German deep-tech company, QuiX Quantum is already actively engaged in the Baden-Württemberg regional innovation ecosystem. The company has established an […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Fully Quantum Algorithm for the 1-dimensional linear Lattice Boltzmann Method
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work represents a significant step forward in quantum computational fluid dynamics by eliminating the need for repeated quantum-classical measurements during simulation, keeping the computation fully quantum until the final readout. The team's validation on a 133-qubit system provides crucial real-world data about how decoherence affects lattice Boltzmann simulations, which model everything from airflow to traffic patterns. While the noise-induced fluctuations highlight current hardware limitations, the fully quantum approach demonstrates a clearer path toward quantum advantage in fluid dynamics once error rates improve. This matters because computational fluid dynamics consumes enormous classical computing resources across industries, making it a prime target for quantum speedups if algorithms like this can scale effectively.

A fully quantum algorithm for solving the one-dimensional linear advection-diffusion equation using the Lattice Boltzmann method as a numerical procedure is presented in this work. We start by presenting a state of the art of the current usage of quantum algorithms for solving ordinary and partial differential equations. We then describe two algorithms for the one-dimensional Lattice Boltzmann method with two degrees of freedom. The first one is an existing hybrid quantum-classical algorithm with measurements at each time step, and the second one is our improved version, viz. a fully quantum algorithm where only one measurement is needed at the end of the algorithm. The fully quantum algorithm is first executed on a quantum simulator and then compared with a classical approach. Subsequently, the fully quantum algorithm is run on a quantum system with 133 qubits to investigate the effect of noise and the depth of the circuit on the output state. We find fluctuations in the final result due to the decoherence noise of the qubits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Detecting basis-dependent hardware errors through spatio-temporal quantum steering
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research introduces a rigorous statistical framework for catching a particularly sneaky class of quantum hardware errors that depend on which measurement basis you use—errors that traditional fidelity metrics can miss entirely. The team demonstrates this on real quantum processors from both superconducting and ion-trap platforms, finding basis-dependent errors even in experiments that appear to work well by conventional measures. This matters because near-term quantum devices are notoriously noisy, and detecting these subtle, basis-dependent failures is crucial for building reliable quantum computers. The work essentially provides hardware developers with a more sensitive diagnostic tool, like upgrading from a basic thermometer to an MRI for spotting problems in quantum processors.

Spatio-temporal quantum steering provides a framework for benchmarking the nonclassicality of general quantum state transfer processes. A central diagnostic is the no-signaling-in-time (NSIT) condition, whose violation can indicate basis-dependent hardware errors. However, finite measurement statistics may also yield apparent violations, thereby obscuring the detection of basis-dependent hardware errors. To address this, we construct a statistical hypothesis test under the null hypothesis that NSIT violations arise solely from statistical fluctuations. Combining the statistical properties of NSIT violation under the null hypothesis with Chebyshev's inequality, we obtain a distribution-free upper bound on the $p$-value without parametric assumptions. We apply this method to two examples. For a single-qubit state-transfer experiment on a superconducting processor, we observe several instances that the NSIT violation is observed and the null hypothesis is simultaneously rejected by a small $p$-value, providing statistical evidence of basis-dependent hardware errors. For a seven-qubit Hayden-Preskill teleportation protocol on IonQ devices, the null hypothesis is also rejected even when the average fidelity exceeds the classical threshold, while the associated nonclassicality measure vanishes. Our results highlight the necessity of statistical hypothesis testing for detecting basis-dependent errors in near-term quantum devices.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Worst-case depth hierarchy for shallow quantum circuits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work establishes a fundamental theoretical result that has eluded quantum complexity theorists: proving that adding even one layer of gates to a shallow quantum circuit provably expands its computational power. The research is particularly notable for developing new mathematical techniques to analyze quantum circuits where traditional methods fall short, essentially creating tools to measure how depth affects a circuit's ability to generate quantum correlations. Beyond the theoretical elegance, these results help map the boundaries of what near-term quantum devices with limited circuit depth can achieve, which has practical implications for understanding the capabilities and limitations of NISQ-era quantum processors. The explicit construction of problems showing unconditional quantum advantage over classical circuits at constant depth adds another rigorous data point to our understanding of where quantum speedups genuinely emerge.

Circuit depth is a central resource in complexity theory. While bounded-depth classical circuits admit well-understood hierarchy theorems, the internal structure of constant-depth quantum computation remains comparatively unexplored. We prove an explicit depth hierarchy theorem for $\mathsf{QNC}^0$. For each $d\ge 12$, we construct a family of two-round interactive problems on which no depth-$(d-1)$ quantum circuit can achieve near-perfect success, regardless of gate set, circuit size, or ancillary qubits. In contrast, we prove that our construction admits realizations by simple bounded fan-in quantum circuits of depth larger than $d$ by a small constant factor. Moreover, all bounded fan-in classical circuits of sublogarithmic depth (in the input size) fail to achieve perfect success on these tasks for every $d$, yielding a hierarchy of problems that show unconditional quantum advantage of $\mathsf{QNC}^0$ over $\mathsf{NC}^0$. A key obstacle is the scarcity of lower bound techniques for quantum circuits. To address this, we develop methods to analyze how depth affects a circuit's ability to realize nonlocal correlations amongst its output qubits in a fine-grained manner. Our approach exploits the correspondence between constraint systems and nonlocal games, translating group-theoretic constructions into rigid operator-valued constraint systems and then into non-local games. In particular, we construct constraint systems whose unique faithful operator-valued solutions require every perfect strategy, and every near-perfect strategy to a fixed precision, to implement multi-controlled phase operations. This reduces to a nonlocal unitary-synthesis problem, yielding depth lower bounds for both shallow quantum and classical circuits. These results show that increasing depth strictly increases computational power within $\mathsf{QNC}^0$, establishing a genuinely quantum hierarchy.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable Graph State Generation with O(1) Local Feedforward in Quantum Networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most fundamental bottlenecks in quantum networking: classical computers simply can't make routing decisions fast enough before qubits lose their fragile quantum states. By reducing local decision complexity to constant time using local measurements and feedforward, the team has found a way to keep network overhead below qubit coherence times regardless of network size, a critical requirement for practical distributed quantum computing. Their demonstration on trapped-ion hardware is particularly significant because it shows the approach works with real-world noise, identifying readout fidelity as the key challenge while proposing concrete solutions through erasure codes and dual-species architectures. The modular star subgraph approach offers a building-block strategy for scaling up quantum networks without exponentially increasing classical overhead, bringing fault-tolerant distributed quantum computing closer to reality.

The development of quantum networks faces a key challenge: the contradiction between probabilistic long-range entanglement generation and finite coherence time. Existing routing protocols typically focus on global state computation or path optimization. As the network scales up, classical delays accumulate and exacerbate decoherence, leading to a decrease in entanglement fidelity. To reduce routing decision delays to levels far below the coherence time of qubits, we propose a protocol based on local measurement and classical feedforward. This protocol reduces the local decision complexity to amortized O(1) level, ensuring that the decision delay is always much smaller than the coherence time of qubits. We map this protocol onto a dual-species trapped-ion platform and perform hybrid simulations. The results show that the proposed protocol performs well in terms of both resource efficiency and time feasibility. Noise analysis indicates that readout fidelity is the main bottleneck of this protocol, but noise suppression can be achieved by employing an erasure transformation in the dual-species architecture, combined with spatial multiplexing and branch independence, thereby ensuring the generation of high-fidelity star subgraphs. This protocol provides a clear path to achieving high-fidelity star subgraphs. These subgraphs can serve as general modules, merging to construct arbitrary subgraphs, providing a feasible solution for future fault-tolerant distributed quantum computing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QNu Labs Takes India’s Quantum Cybersecurity to Bharat Innovates 2026, Signs Research Deal with Eindhoven University of Technology
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights India's growing ambitions in quantum cybersecurity as QNu Labs showcases their quantum key distribution technology at a major international innovation summit. The company's new research partnership with Eindhoven University of Technology demonstrates how Indian quantum startups are building crucial academic bridges to advance their technologies on the global stage. QNu Labs' presence at Bharat Innovates 2026 reflects India's strategic push to position itself as a quantum technology leader, particularly in the critical area of quantum-safe communications where early commercial applications are already emerging. This kind of international collaboration and visibility is essential for quantum startups to scale their technologies and compete in the rapidly evolving global quantum market.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QNu Labs is in Nice, France this week at Bharat Innovates 2026 a global platform organised by India’s Ministry of Education and the French Ministry to connect deep tech founders with global enterprises, investors, research institutions, and policymakers to showcase their breakthrough innovation to the world. The company is one […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Reconstruction of detector error model for quantum error correction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles a critical bottleneck in scaling fault-tolerant quantum computers: accurately identifying the complex, correlated errors that plague real quantum hardware. The researchers' CAHR algorithm addresses a significant weakness in current methods, which often generate spurious error patterns that don't actually exist while missing real physical correlations. By demonstrating reconstruction without false positives on both surface codes and the more challenging color codes, this approach could substantially improve decoder performance on actual quantum processors. The discovery of the "variance cascade" phenomenon is particularly valuable, as it explains why extracting precise error rates becomes harder in dense codes and points toward a practical two-stage strategy for real-world quantum error correction.

Fault-tolerant quantum computing fundamentally relies on the accurate characterization of circuit-level noise to optimize decoding algorithms. However, extracting complex multi-body error correlations remains challenging. Contemporary greedy inference algorithms can suffer from statistical distortion, discarding true physical mechanisms while introducing many unphysical false positives. Here, we introduce the Correlation-Analysis-based Hypergraph Reconstruction (CAHR) algorithm, a globally consistent framework to invert experimental syndrome statistics directly into discrete physical hypergraphs. By coupling exact algebraic correlation equations with a top-down concurrent-pruning strategy, CAHR recovers the fault topology without false positives for both $d=5$ rotated surface codes and dense 8-body 2D color codes in our benchmark settings. Furthermore, we show that exact continuous parameter extraction in dense codes is limited by a \textit{variance cascade}, where absolute statistical variance accumulates linearly from high- to low-degree mechanisms. This motivates a two-stage inference paradigm: utilizing CAHR to extract the fault topology, followed by continuous probability optimization. This provides a practical approach for characterizing and decoding highly correlated noise in realistic quantum hardware.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Efficient Magic State Factory Via Transversal Non-Clifford Gate
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of the trickiest bottlenecks in fault-tolerant quantum computing: efficiently producing the "magic states" needed to perform complex quantum operations beyond basic Clifford gates. By directly comparing two leading approaches—code switching and magic-state cultivation—through comprehensive end-to-end simulations, the researchers provide quantum engineers with concrete data about which method works better under different conditions. The development of a lattice surgery protocol connecting the doubled color code and rotated surface code is particularly valuable, as it enables more flexible architecture designs. Most intriguingly, their hybrid protocols combining phase-kickback checks with transversal non-Clifford gates could represent a new pathway toward more resource-efficient quantum computers that spend less overhead on generating these essential computational resources.

Magic-state preparation is a central component of fault-tolerant quantum computing. Recent theoretical and experimental successes in code-switch-based magic-state preparation have underscored the promise of these methods for quantum error correction. Similarly, magic-state cultivation has likewise been demonstrated in both numerical and experimental settings. However, a thorough comparison between magic-state cultivation and code-switch-based magic-state factories is still missing. In this work, we carry out end-to-end simulations of magic-state preparation using code switching and compare its resource requirements and performance against magic-state cultivation. As part of this analysis, we develop a lattice-surgery protocol for transfer between the doubled color code and the rotated surface code. We extend the complete code-switching protocol to the $d=5$ doubled color code and perform the corresponding end-to-end simulations. Finally, we propose two fault-tolerant magic-state preparation protocols that combine phase-kickback checks with a transversal non-Clifford gate.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Enhancing Quantum Machine Learning with Anyons
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work breaks new ground by demonstrating that anyonic statistics—exotic quantum particles that exist between bosons and fermions—can enhance machine learning performance beyond what's achievable with conventional quantum approaches. While most quantum machine learning research has focused on leveraging superposition and entanglement, this team shows that the fractional exchange statistics of anyons access previously unexplored regions of quantum feature space, leading to measurably better learning outcomes across multiple benchmarks.
